title: 4-7-8 breath for sleep
subtitle: A five-minute breath practice for the wired bedtime body, drawn on Andrew Weil's adaptation of pranayama.
excerpt: A guided five-minute breath practice for bedtime. Inhale for four counts, hold for seven, exhale for eight. Eight rounds. The lengthened exhale signals the parasympathetic nervous system that the day is over. Used lying down, eyes closed, just before sleep.
sourceNotes: 4-7-8 breath was popularised in modern form by Andrew Weil (b. 1942), drawing on traditional yogic pranayama breathing practices, specifically the lengthened-exhale family of practices in hatha yoga. Bedtime adaptation drawn from Day 9 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Soothe the body with breath".

content.text: A guided five-minute breath practice for bedtime. Inhale for four counts, hold for seven, exhale for eight. Eight rounds. The lengthened exhale carries information to the nervous system that the day is closing, the parasympathetic system responds by slowing the heart and softening the body.
New to body-based meditation? Read
body-based meditation
first.
The practice
Setting up
Lie in bed, on your back. A pillow under the knees if the lower back is tender. Tongue resting against the roof of the mouth, just behind the front teeth, this is the start position for the practice.
Exhale fully through the mouth, making a soft sound as the air leaves. Then close the mouth.
The breath
One round of the practice:
Inhale through the nose for four counts.
Slow, quiet. The belly rises before the chest does.
Hold the breath for seven counts.
The body stays still.
Exhale through the mouth for eight counts.
The same soft sound as the opening exhale. The body softens with the air leaving.

That is one round. The count is the structure, not the goal, if four / seven / eight is too long for your lungs, scale to a smaller version (3 / 4 / 5, or whatever works) keeping the same ratio. The slow exhale is the working part of the practice.
Do eight rounds. By round four most bodies have slowed; by round eight the body is often near sleep.
Coming back
If you are still awake after eight rounds, let the breath return to its natural rhythm. Stay with the eyes closed, on your back. Sleep usually follows.
If you drift off before round eight, the practice has done its work. The count was the scaffolding; the slow exhale was the bridge.
Where this practice comes from

4-7-8 breath was popularised in modern form by Andrew Weil (b. 1942), who adapted the specific count from traditional yogic pranayama. Pranayama is the breath-control practice tradition within hatha yoga, with documented lineage going back to at least the fifteenth-century Hatha Yoga Pradipika. The lengthened-exhale family of practices appears across many of pranayama's specific patterns. The bedtime adaptation used here is drawn from Day 9 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
